zh.lifeSummary: 當Agnes Johnstone Alexander於1413年出生在英國英格蘭貝德福爾特拉爾 貝德福爾時，她的父親Robert Alexander14歲，母親Janet Dunbar23歲。她於大約1435年與Charles Henry Tilley在英國結婚。他們至少是1個兒子和1個女兒的父母。她去世於1482年6月1日在她的家鄉享年69歲，埋葬在英國英格蘭貝德福爾特拉爾 貝德福爾。

nameMeanings.name: Alexander
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Scottish, English, German, and Dutch: from the personal name Alexander, classical Greek Alexandros, which probably originally meant ‘repulser of men (i.e. of the enemy)’, from alexein ‘to repel’ + andros, genitive of anēr ‘man’. Its popularity in the Middle Ages was due mainly to the Macedonian conqueror, Alexander the Great (356–323 BC ) - or rather to the hero of the mythical versions of his exploits that gained currency in the so-called Alexander Romances. The name was also borne by various early Christian saints, including a patriarch of Alexandria (c. 250–326 AD ), whose main achievement was condemning the Arian heresy. The Gaelic form of the personal name is Alasdair, which has given rise to a number of Scottish and Irish patronymics, for example McAllister . Alexander is a common personal name in Scotland, often representing an Anglicized form of the Gaelic name. In North America, the English form of the surname has absorbed cognates from other languages, e.g. Spanish Alejandro , Italian Alessandro , Arabic or Assyrian/Chaldean Iskandar and Iskander , and their derivatives, e.g. Greek patronymic Alexandropoulos.
Jewish: from the adopted personal name Alexander (see 1 above) or shortened from the eastern Ashkenazic (originally Slavic) patronymics Aleksandrovich or Alexandrowicz.

History: A number of Scotch-Irish families of this name landed at New York in the early 18th century. By 1746, six of them were established in NC. Others came in through Philadelphia, for example Archibald Alexander, who came from Londonderry in northern Ireland in 1736 and established himself in VA. — The Revolutionary general William Alexander (1726–83) was always known as ‘Lord Sterling’ to his compatriots, although his claim to the title was denied by the College of Arms in London. His father, James Alexander, was a Jacobite who had fled to New York after the failure of the Jacobite rising in 1715. The claim to the title arose in connection with their ancestor Sir William Alexander, a courtier and poet at the court of King James VI of Scotland (James I of England), who created him Earl of Stirling in 1633.

variants.name: Alley
meanings: English: from the Middle English personal name Ally, Alli, as forms such as Johannes filius Alli (Norfolk, 1205) make clear. It may be a pet form of Alexander , Alan , Alice , or Alison , or a survival of Old Danish Alli, Old Swedish Alle, found as Alli in the Domesday Book.
In some cases also an altered form of French Hallé (see Halle 2) or of some other similar (like-sounding) surname.
Variant of   Allee   2, a surname of French origin.

variants.name: Aleck
meanings: English: perhaps a short form of the personal name   Alexander  .

variants.name: Alejandro
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Juan, Jose, Luis, Alfredo, Manuel, Miguel, Pablo, Angel, Armando, Felipe, Gerardo, Jesus.
Spanish: from the personal name Alejandro, an equivalent of Alexander . Compare De Alejandro .

variants.name: Alessandro
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Nicola, Biagio, Domenic, Natale, Rocco, Salvatore, Angelo, Antonio, Carlo, Carmello, Carmin, Enzo.
Italian: from the personal name Alessandro, from classical Greek Alexandros (see Alexander ).

variants.name: Alexandrou
meanings: Greek: patronymic from the genitive case of the personal name Alexandros (see Alexander ).

variants.name: McAllister
meanings: Scottish and northern Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Alasdair ‘son of Alasdair’, the Gaelic form of the personal name Alexander .
History: MacAlister is the name of a Scottish Highland clan claiming descent from Alasdair Mòr Mac Dòmhnaill, a younger son of Domhnall mac Raghnaill, founder of Clan Donald (see McDonald ).

variants.name: Alexandre
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French/Haitian Pierre, Michel, Jacques, Julien, Rodolphe, Andre, Cecile, Colette, Dominique, Eunide, Francoise, Georges.  Spanish Jose, Fernando, Adela, Amandio, Amarante, Ana, Beatriz, Carlos, Claudio, Consuelo, Isidro, Joaquina.  Portuguese Joaquim, Aderito, Serafim.
French, Portuguese, West Indian (mainly Haiti), and Mauritian: from the personal name Alexandre (see Alexander ).

variants.name: Aleksanyan
meanings: Armenian: patronymic from the personal name Aleksan, a short form of Aleksander (see Alexander ). This form (transliteration) of the surname is found mainly in Armenia and Russia.

variants.name: Alexandra
meanings: English: variant of Alexander , apparently from the feminine form of the personal name. — Note: It is possible that in the population figure published by the US Census Bureau (which dropped from 644 in the year 2000 to 440 in the year 2010) the female personal name Alexandra of some American bearers is also counted as a surname. Some of these bearers may be from countries, such as Indonesia, where (hereditary) surnames are not in general use.

variants.name: Aleksandrov
meanings: Russian, Bulgarian, Macedonian, and Serbian: patronymic from the personal name Aleksandr, Aleksandar (see Alexander ). Compare Alexandrov .

nameMeanings.name: Agnes
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Latinized version of the Greek name Hagnē, from the feminine form of the adjective hagnos ‘pure, holy’. This was the name of a young Roman virgin martyred in the persecutions instigated by the Roman emperor Diocletian in ad 303 . She became a very popular saint in the Middle Ages. Her name was early associated with Latin agnus ‘lamb’, leading to the consistent dropping of the initial H- and to her representation in art accompanied by a lamb. The colloquial form Annes led to some confusion with Ann(e) in earlier centuries. Frequent in the medieval period, the name was revived in the 19th century, and has been especially popular in Scotland. See also Annis .

nameMeanings.name: Johnstone
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Transferred use of the surname, derived originally from a place in Scotland (notably the one in Annandale) named in Old English as ‘John's settlement’, or a variant of   Johnson .

en.lifeSummary: When Agnes Johnstone Alexander was born in 1413, in Central Bedfordshire, Bedfordshire, England, United Kingdom, her father, Robert Alexander, was 14 and her mother, Janet Dunbar, was 23. She married Charles Henry Tilley about 1435, in England. They were the parents of at least 1 son and 1 daughter. She died on 1 June 1482, in her hometown, at the age of 69, and was buried in Central Bedfordshire, Bedfordshire, England, United Kingdom.

ko.lifeSummary: Agnes Johnstone Alexander(이)가 1413년 영국 잉글랜드 베드퍼드실르 세트럴 베드퍼드실르에서 태어났을 때, 그녀의 아버지 Robert Alexander(은)는 14세였고, 그녀의 어머니 Janet Dunbar(은)는 23세였다. 그녀는 대략 1435년 영국에서 Charles Henry Tilley(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 한 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그녀는 1482년 6월 1일 그녀의 고향에서 69세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 잉글랜드 베드퍼드실르 세트럴 베드퍼드실르에 묻혔다.

Она вышла замуж за человека по имени Charles Henry Tilley около 1435 г. в местности Англия. У них был по крайней мере 1 сын и 1 дочь. Она умерла 1 июня 1482 г. в её родном городе в возрасте 69 лет и была похоронена в местности кентраль бедфордшир, бедфордшир, Англия, Великобритания.
